sentences.text: What do you want--
Well, I just must go back to that and mention what a wonderful person my mother was and I said, she was most hospitable person.
And we haven't been very wealthy and, you know, it wasn't everything of plenty like it is today.
But my mother always did when-- whoever popped in-- and it wasn't by appointments like very often in Australia.
It was just very spontaneous, and family and friend just popped in.
And my mother never, never did make feel anybody that didn't receive them empty-handed, you know.
She always did have things to offer.

And I remember very often-- and I was angry about it at time-- my mother would happily give so many dinners, which was for her, she would happily give away to the cousin or whoever came in unexpected.
Well, yes it was-- and even after the Depression, you know, you only cook for a certain amount of people and but so she didn't mind at all.
She was always happy to give whatever she has.
She was wonderful person, I must say.
Yes, especially with my mother.
Yes.
Yes.

Well, as I said, when the first soldiers arrive in-- German soldiers arrived in tanks and looking with their helmets and were so powerful and it was so-- do I-- should I repeat myself and say they did look indeed like from another planet, you know?
It was completely unknown to us technology and motoring and the uniforming.
And it was a very scary feeling, very scary.
Right from the start they was taking people, specially they spotted easily all those Orthodox people.
They took them to work to sweep the streets.
They was offensive.
They would take them by the beards and, you know, shake them
Hm?
Yeah, I did.
Well, mind you, we didn't want to witness too much.
We mostly observed wherever we came to be, wherever it was possible to be invisible, you understand?
Because you have been the next victim if you have been visible, right?
They came-- well, the war started in September, and that was towards the-- it was in September 19-- 1939.
Well, there was a talk about the Corridor, [BACKGROUND NOISE] you know?
There was some discussion about the Corridor, which is part of Gdansk and you know.
And Prussia was sort of a part of Germany, and they called it the Corridor, yeah?
And there was talking that the time wasn't good, and we felt that something would happen soon.
It was the feeling, as I recall.
But it happen-- they were so quick about gaining the territory, and it was terrible.
Of course, yes, because you see, before the war there was a lot of people from different parts of Germany and Austria which was expelled from Germany and came to Krakow.
There was a lot of German Jews which did-- well, I can't-- I don't know.
Some of them, I don't think they came voluntary.
They have been sent to Krakow.
Well, of course.
We knew it was a terrible time.
We knew about the Kristallnacht.
We know that the Jews was persecuted and unwanted in Germany.
And the German people was absolutely so patriotic, you know?
It was really if ever you can say there was good German, they identified themselves-- a lot of them was very assimilated.
And if anything, I think their priority was to identify themself as a German, and then very much secondary as the Jews.
It was so patriotic.
I remember one girl-- one woman-- young woman and her husband, they brought the-- in a box, in a matchbox, they brought some German earth with them.
You know, they absolutely-- I don't know.
They were so-- well, who could have dreamed that such a thing can happen?
I mean, it was absolutely beyond us, you know?
Well, I-- then ghetto was formed.
Just let me think.
I think ghetto was formed 1940.
Don't hold me to it.
Some dates, you know, in those unhappy memories, I really did try to separate so much, and I am not very good with dates.
But maybe somebody else will be more helpful about the dates.
But I think the ghetto was formed end of 1940.
And then the Kazimierz, that was the part where the Jewish people did live, they did have to all-- they was evacuated and come to Podgorze, where actually was my-- where I did live before.
And, of course, they crowd us very much into a ghetto, which was-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] it was isolated-- very-- physically with some-- what you call it?
Wires, yes?
And they crowd three families in or four families in one place where before one family did work.
In your house?
Did you stay in that house?
Well, all my aunts and all-- they crowded into our place, but I married 1940 in August and then I was put together-- we didn't-- we didn't live together, my husband.
It was my family.
We live with another family, and then later, again, with another family.
There was one, two, three rooms, and there was for families living there.
And it's amazing the spirit of the people.
You know, in the beginning it was-- well, it was gradually worse and worse.
But the spirit was such that we did keep up the spirit by talking-- saying-- telling jokes through the night, the young people and having-- we cried at the best time for ourself we could, right?
Well, I would socialize with the people mainly because it was-- as I remember, you couldn't go out after dark and things like this, so mostly it was the people that was neighboring or living with us.
We did try to do the best for ourselves--
What, you mean for amusement?
Well, we was often cooking together or we was singing together or we was trying to make the most out of a very bad time.
Yes, it was a time of fear for the future.
It was a time to be very uncertain.
And I married in August, as I said, 1940.
I was very young and the tendency there to marry-- a lot of young girls did marry because, silly as it sounds today, it was such attitude we didn't want to die as virgin.
[LAUGHS]
Today that is very stupid, but that was the morality then.
And a girl who wasn't virgin when she married was did have the idea that her husband would not respect her, you know?
And so really this way I think that most girls was very moral.
Yes.
And--
I married two-- there was two weddings.
And I have some very unhappy memories on the day of my wedding because I went to a hairdresser, and I did try to look good for the wedding.
And I was called to work to mend the socks on my way home by a German soldiers.
And I was absolutely frantic because I said, you know, people-- I'm going to my wedding.
People are waiting for-- my friends was waiting.
And they laughed and they didn't believe me or they just tried to be horrible.
And they kept me there for about two hours.
So I did come-- I was hysterical, I remember.
When I-- finally they let me go, people was dispersing and didn't know what happened to me, and I don't think I ever was dressed as quickly in my life as I was to my wedding.
[LAUGHS] I think it was exactly one minute when I put what I did have on.
It was only a suit and some-- and I think my eyes must've been very red.
And the people who was there still-- So what was the actual wedding like?
Well, the wedding was-- [LAUGHS] it was a double wedding.
My husband's sister did marry and [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] yes, and we did marry.
It was a double wedding, and it was a very poor wedding, I must say.
Well, it was sad because I was late, and they knew what happened, and you know.
And-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] it was more like a formality than a happy occasion, do you understand?
Anyway--
Yes, I-- we was married by a rabbi.
It was-- it was already in the ghetto, yes.
Well, I did-- I didn't-- I don't think they did have a knowledge of such a event, you know?
I mean, they didn't-- I don't think it did have to be declared or anything like that.
So we just married.
[LAUGHS]
No.
I met my husband when I was 14, and he carried my books to school for me.
And he actually come from another town, but his parents did live in Krakow.
And we met in a park during a stormy weather.
I was frightened of storm, and he spoke to me, said-- because I was running home because it start a big thunder.
And I was scared, and he said, why you running away?
It's beautiful.
The storm is beautiful.
You shouldn't be afraid, you know?
And I'd think, oh, isn't he heroic?
[LAUGHS] I did think he's very brave by not being afraid of such a bad thunder.
And then he was away again, and then he came back.
But I knew him almost six years before we married, yes.
You married at 18 did you?
Yes.
Yes.
Sorry.
One, two-- was three families living together, three families.
Was crowded into.
They're not happy memories, really.
Very unhappy memories.
And then it came the liquidation of the ghetto and--
Well, it was a big smuggling going on because we wasn't allowed to freely bring the food to the ghetto.
I did work then.
I did work in a place called Optima.
I was sewing uniforms for the German soldiers.
And there was a soup served there.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And--
Well, we did have to just sew the uniform as much as possible to output and but those actually was quite lucky and sheltered which did have these jobs.
I wasn't married then, I don't think, when I did work in this Optima.
It was before I was married.
It was scarce.
The food was scarce, but at that stage I did not find it was a great hunger for me.
Somehow the families together and all this, we somehow managed.
And as the time went on, it was worse and worse, of course.
And then come the big shock of ghetto to be liquidated and first the transport.
They was catching people for what they did call transports.
And I was scared.
I did live in the same building, but two story higher than my mother and my sister and my family and my grandmother.
And then when the transport was-- I heard-- when the people was called to transport, I heard that my mother and my sister was in this.
They was congregating them on a big place together surrounded by police and not to escape.
And I remember this incident very vividly.
When I did find out that they are in a transport, I was very disturbed.
And I went to this place, and there was a lot of police-- SS people there and Gestapo people.
And I went to this what then was the chief of the Gestapo in this-- for this aktion.
He was with a dog and, I don't know.
I wasn't scared.
All I did think, how to take them out from the transfer.
And I approach him, and he look at me and he spoke to me.
And, of course, the other people was then daring to try to approach him as well.
And for some strange reason he would talk to me and shoot in my presence everybody who did want to approach him.
And that was a terrible experience for me.
And he took me what it was called then a [NON-ENGLISH], which was this police station.
And he-- because those people who did have the stamp-- what was called [NON-ENGLISH].
And that means some-- it was a document which identify you with a allowance to stay in Krakow in the ghetto.
And this stamp was so important because without the stamp, you didn't have the chance to stay.
He gave me the chance-- the stamp, and I took my sister and my mother from the transport.
I brought them home, and I said, please don't go out now because they was short of taking the-- there was a quota.
So many people did have to be in the transport, and they were short of the people to transport.
And so I begged them not to come out.
They did live with my grandmother together and the Gestapo was running and taking all the old people, you know.
So my mother and my sister did think it would be safe to transport my grandmother to hospital.
And they did go out to try to get the what they called a-- oh god, the thing what they would put sick people on.
What do you call that?
Stretcher.
Stretcher.
Stretcher.
Sorry.
See?
I mustn't get too excited because then the words are missing.
And well they did get the stretcher.

And they-- my sister and my mother went to hospital to put my grandmother there and when the hospital was taken all to the transport later, but they did think, being human people, that the sick people would be not taken to the transport.
Anyway it was-- we didn't know what the transport.
They said the transport is to take people to work to a different place, right?
Anyway-- Did you believe that?
Well, we were skeptical, but we didn't have evidence, you see?
We were skeptical because how could-- we knew what happened to the German Jews, we knew-- so we knew it's a bad time and bad news for the Jews.
Anyway, and then my grandmother was taken with the whole hospital to the transport or whatever.
And when-- and my mother and my sister was called again because they was at that stage taking everybody wherever they could because the quota wasn't completed.
And when this transport went and-- well, I went.
I came out from my place, and somebody told me, oh I did see your mother and your sister in a transport.
I went downstairs where I did live, and they wasn't there and they went.
[SOBBING] I couldn't do anything about it anymore.
And I-- what I understand, they went to Treblinka which was the extermination.
They gassed them or whatever.
That's the last time I, I-- I didn't know where they went.
Oh, I knew it wasn't good because I understand they put them in this wagons.
They was telling terrible stories.
They-- I don't know.
They put some calcium or something which was boiling, and they must have gone through such a hell before they--
I don't know.
There was some fumes in the wagons, what I understand.
So there are a lot stories.
I don't know how-- I couldn't believe they was talking about.
And my father also went separate with another transport.
I don't know.
I don't know.
Oh, well, [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] well, transports was done what I understand the way that-- first the older people did go, then the families did go.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] I wasn't in hiding but-- oh, about the stamps.
My husband didn't have the stamp, and he wasn't allowed to stay in ghetto.
So I did try to save him, and I went to this what was the German quarters.
There was one Czech woman there, I remember.
[NON-ENGLISH] was her name.
And I somehow knew her.
She was working-- she was friendly, and she opened the window.
It was like [NON-ENGLISH], which was like saying half a story, right?
And she opened the window and pulled me by the hands to try to give me the stamp for my husband.

And in this instant when I was inside there was [NON-ENGLISH], I think, was the man, the Gestapo man who was-- and he was in trouble because there was a great pressure on him to give-- not to give so many stamps, right?
Anyway, he came in.
He was very distressed, and the last thing I remember he hit me very hard over the head because I was wrongly placed there at the time, you know?
He hit me over the head, and I don't know.
I did fell unconscious.
I can't tell you how long.
I only know when I did wake up I was very dizzy, and I remember I did have the [NON-ENGLISH] for my husband with a stamp and I saved him from going.
But I must have been there for quite some time because when I come to, there wasn't many people there anymore, you know?
There was nobody there.
I just made my way.
So this [INAUDIBLE].
And what happened to you after?
Sometime, you know, when my-- I don't know.
For some-- sometime when I have a pressure in my head, which I sometime have, you know, when I don't feel so good, I always think maybe because of what happened to me then.
But--
Well, that's-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] gradually the ghetto supposed to be liquidated.
And I remember it was several transport.
They arrive and I see them walking the whole families, brave people not knowing what with their little packets or whatever they did think they need to take.
Wonderful, wonderful people, proud people with this transport going-- so brave, you know?
Well, I remember distinguish one family, and they was bosses of my husband.
They did have a big business in well, it's a motoring and hardware and, you know, big business it was.
And I remember him.
He was a lovely man with his wife and his children.
And he was-- I did see him in a transport walking so proud, you know?
Like the idea, they can't get me sort of thing, you know?
Not knowing, because he didn't survive.
Terrible memories.
And how many of that kind of aktion was there?
What I recall, must've been three or four transports.
And of-- and then, oh look.
Can we have a little interval before I have to go on because it comes now to that little girl, my cousin and how she-- can you--
Can you?
Because I need a bit more water and I--
